expected_output.explanation: A response that: (1) says CEO sold $2.1M without jargon, (2) mentions 14 months since last sale, (3) says the sale wasn't planned/scheduled, (4) cites the ~12% average historical drop, (5) tells the SMCI holder why this matters to them

A response that: (1) mentions $12M sale without jargon, (2) emphasizes 22 months since last transaction (nearly 2 years), (3) notes 40% position reduction, (4) cites 8.7% historical drop, (5) clearly addresses an NVDA holder

A response that: (1) notes this was a scheduled/planned sale (not alarming), (2) contextualizes the large dollar amount against small position reduction (2%), (3) does NOT catastrophize since it's routine, (4) is calm and reassuring to a TSLA holder

A response that: (1) notes CFO sold $637K, (2) flags 25% position reduction as significant, (3) mentions 8 months since last sale, (4) connects to same ~12% historical pattern as CEO sale, (5) signals this is a second insider selling (more concerning)

Microsoft’s CEO sold shares on his own, not through a routine pre-planned setup, so that deserves a bit more attention for someone who holds MSFT. It had been 12 months since his last transaction, which makes the timing stand out, and the sale trimmed his stake by 5%, right at the boundary of a modest reduction. Since there’s no historical pattern here, the main takeaway is that this is a meaningful insider sale to watch, because it can hint at how the top executive feels about the stock.

Broadcom's CEO sold $42.5M of stock, but it was part of a planned routine sale, so this is not an alarm bell. He last traded just 1 month ago, and his stake was only cut by 3%, which keeps the move fairly small despite the large dollar amount. The historical pattern you provided is based on similar unscheduled insider sales, with an average 30-day drop of 4.7%, but that doesn't change the fact this sale looks routine; if you hold AVGO, you should care because a CEO sale is still worth watching, even when it's pre-planned.

Ruth Porat sold shares at Alphabet, but this was part of a routine plan, so it reads as calm rather than alarming. It’s only been 1 month since her last transaction, and her stake was cut by 4%, which is a small move even with the large dollar amount. The pattern you provided is about similar unscheduled insider sales, so it doesn’t change the fact that this sale looks planned. If you hold GOOGL, you should care because it shows insider selling is happening, but this one does not point to a strong warning sign.

A director sold about $2.1M of Adobe shares, and this was not part of a routine plan, so it reads as a more meaningful insider move. It has been 12 months since this insider’s last transaction, which makes the timing stand out even though the stake was cut by only 4%; since more than one insider is selling, holders should pay a little extra attention. Similar unscheduled insider sales have been followed by an average 6.2% drop over 30 days, so if you hold ADBE, this is worth watching because it can hint at how confident insiders feel.
